narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with a solid upper-middle-class family background, you've put significant effort into your undergraduate studies and LSAT preparation, achieving an undergraduate GPA of 3.30—which is actually a bit above the typical student's 3.21—and an LSAT score of 34. Your plan to attend law school full-time aligns with the vast majority of applicants, who also choose that path. However, the admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated your risk of failure at 56.6%, tipping the scales just over the threshold that led to your application's rejection. This prediction was driven primarily by your academic decile rankings from earlier years: your Year 3 decile of 2 was the biggest factor pushing the model toward forecasting a bar failure, as it stands notably below the average of around 5.5 and signals potential challenges in sustaining high performance. On a more positive note, your undergraduate GPA had the strongest counterbalancing influence, pulling the prediction toward success because it's stronger than what most applicants bring. Your family income level nudged things slightly toward the riskier side compared to typical profiles, while your LSAT score offered some helpful but lesser support in the model's eyes, given it's under the average of 36.7. Your Year 1 decile of 4 and full-time status played smaller roles, with the former adding a minor push toward failure and the latter providing a tiny boost. Overall, compared to typical students who get approved, your profile shows strengths in recent undergraduate work but is held back by those earlier weaker school deciles, especially in Year 3, which outweighed your other positives. While this is disappointing, it's not the end of the road—consider retaking courses or building a stronger academic record to improve those decile rankings, perhaps through post-baccalaureate programs or additional standardized testing. You might also explore part-time options or schools with more holistic reviews that weigh your full-time commitment and family stability more favorably. Keep pushing forward; many applicants in similar situations turn things around with targeted improvements. We're rooting for you to ace that bar exam on the first try.
